content: If a relational decomposition is not dependency-preserving, which one of the following relational operators will be executed more frequently in order to maintain the dependencies?
explanation:
If a relational decomposition is not dependency-preserving, which relational operator will be executed more frequently to maintain the dependencies?

Step 1: Recall what dependency-preserving decomposition means

When we decompose a relation $ R $ into $ R_1, R_2, ..., R_n $:

  • The decomposition is dependency-preserving if all functional dependencies (FDs) from $ R $ can be enforced by enforcing dependencies only on each $ R_i $, without needing to join the relations back together.

Step 2: What happens if it is not dependency-preserving?

If the decomposition is not dependency-preserving, some FDs span multiple decomposed relations.

That means you cannot test or enforce those dependencies by looking at individual relations alone.

To check or enforce such dependencies, you must:

  • Join the decomposed relations,

  • Then test whether the combined tuples satisfy the original dependency.

Step 3: Determine which operator is involved

The operator required to combine decomposed relations is the JOIN operator.

So, if a decomposition is not dependency-preserving, the join operation must be performed more frequently to maintain and enforce the dependencies.

✅ Correct Answer: Option C — Join

content:
Consider the following two relations, named Customer and Person, in a

database:

Person (

aadhaar CHAR(12) PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(32));

Customer (

name VARCHAR(32), email VARCHAR(32) PRIMARY KEY, phone CHAR(10), aadhaar CHAR(12), FOREIGN KEY (aadhaar) REFERENCES Person(aadhaar)); Which of the following statements is/are correct?

options.content: aadhaar is a candidate key in the Customer relation
phone can be NULL in the Customer relation
aadhaar is a candidate key in the Person relation
aadhaar can be NULL in the Person relation

explanation:
Aadhar is foreign key in customer.

Phenp can be null as nothing is mentioned, aadhar is a primary key and all primary keys are candidate key.

Aadhar cannot be null as it is primary key. Hence, the correct option is (B, C).

content:
Consider a database relation $R$ with attributes ABCDEFG , and having the following functional dependencies:

$$ \mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{BCEF} \quad \mathrm{E} \rightarrow \mathrm{DG} \quad \mathrm{BC} \rightarrow \mathrm{~A} $$

Which of the following statements is/are correct?

options.content: A is the only candidate key of R
A, BC are the candidate keys of R
A, BC, E are the candidate keys of R
Relation R is not in Boyce-Codd Normal Form (BCNF)

explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & R(A B C D E F G) \\ & A \rightarrow B C E F \\ & E \rightarrow D G \\ & B C \rightarrow A \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & (A)^{+}=\{A B C D E F G\} \\ & (B C)^{+}=\{A B C D E F G\} \end{aligned} $$

Only A and BC are candidate key and it is not in BCNF as

$$ E \rightarrow D G $$

In this functional dependency $E$ is not a super key.

Hence, the correct option is (B, D).
